target: SFOS 22.0.0.411 deep code audit (mode 6000 + deserialization + SSO)
description: Parallel deep-dive analysis: (1) Mode 6000 validate_hammer_token, (2) Java deserialization surface, (3) SSO/SAML authentication flow

findings.title: SSOAdminServlet Missing Return After Auth Failure -- Session Created Despite User Not Found
findings.category: CWE-841: Improper Enforcement of Behavioral Workflow
findings.description: In SSOAdminServlet, when sessionBean.getUserID() == -1 (SSO user not found in local DB), the code sends a redirect to login.jsp but does NOT return. Execution falls through to create an HTTP session with the sessionBean (containing userID=-1), store the access token, and set session attributes. A client that ignores the redirect retains a server-side session with a valid JSESSIONID. The same pattern exists for schedule restriction failure (returnedStatus == 201 with failed schedule check).
findings.evidence: SSOAdminServlet.java lines 132-136: if (sessionBean.getUserID() == -1) { sendRedirect(...); } // NO RETURN -- falls through to session creation at lines 137-151
findings.impact: Session created for non-existent user (userID=-1). Likely fails downstream ACL checks, but the session itself is valid. Combined with schedule restriction bypass, a time-restricted SSO admin can still get a session created. Needs live testing to determine if the userID=-1 session grants any access.

findings.title: SSOAdminServlet X-Forwarded-Email Header Trust -- Privilege Escalation
findings.description: SSOAdminServlet reads X-Forwarded-Email directly from the client HTTP request to perform user lookup. The only guard is validateSSOCookie() which checks the oauth2-proxy session cookie against 127.0.0.1:65020/oauth2/auth. If a low-privilege SSO user authenticates legitimately, they can then set X-Forwarded-Email to a super-admin's email address. If oauth2-proxy does not overwrite this header when proxying to the upstream Jetty, the attacker gets a session as the targeted admin.
findings.evidence: SSOAdminServlet line 51: UserBean userBean = new UserHelper().getRecordByEmail(request.getHeader('X-Forwarded-Email'), sqlReader). No validation that X-Forwarded-Email matches the authenticated SSO session.
findings.impact: Privilege escalation from any SSO-authenticated user to any admin whose email exists in the system. Requires SSO to be configured (not default). Needs live testing to verify oauth2-proxy header behavior.
findings.notes: NEEDS LIVE VALIDATION. The exploitability depends on whether oauth2-proxy overwrites X-Forwarded-Email for all proxied requests. Standard oauth2-proxy sets this header from the authenticated token, but if the upstream (Jetty) is also directly accessible on port 4444, the client controls this header.

findings.title: SSOAdminServlet Schedule Restriction Bypass -- Missing Return
findings.category: CWE-841: Improper Enforcement of Behavioral Workflow
findings.description: When the access-manager returns code 201 (success with restrictions) and the schedule check fails, the code sets a 503 status in the response JSON and sends a redirect, but does NOT return. Execution continues to prepareSSOSessionBean() and full session creation. A schedule-restricted admin user can still get a valid admin session.
findings.evidence: SSOAdminServlet lines 118-131: schedule check failure sends redirect but falls through to session creation
findings.impact: Time-based access controls on SSO admin accounts are ineffective. An admin blocked by schedule restriction can still authenticate and get full access.

findings.title: Mode 6000 (validate_hammer_token) -- Dead Mock Validator with Substring Matching
findings.category: CWE-798: Use of Hard-coded Credentials (dead code)
findings.description: CentralHelper contains a mockCentralTokenValidation() method that reads /var/central_token and uses String.contains() for token validation (substring match, not equality). This method is NEVER CALLED in the production code path -- token validation is always delegated to Sophos Central's cloud API. However, if this method were ever activated for debugging, any substring of the stored token would be accepted.
findings.evidence: CentralHelper.mockCentralTokenValidation(): reads /var/central_token, checks fileToken.contains(token). Dead code -- not referenced in production flow.
findings.impact: None in current code. If activated for debugging, trivially bypassable -- empty string or single character would match. Design note only.

findings.title: X-Forwarded-For IP Spoofing Bypasses SSO IP Restrictions
findings.description: SSOAdminServlet and the broader authentication framework use HFHelper.getClientIP() which trusts the X-Forwarded-For header. IP-based access restrictions on admin SSO login can be bypassed by setting a spoofed X-Forwarded-For header. RequestCheckFilter.isValidRequest() validates the header format (valid IPs) but does not prevent spoofing.
findings.evidence: HFHelper.getClientIP() reads X-FORWARDED-FOR directly. SSOAdminServlet line 77 passes this to access-manager. IP restriction checks at lines 125, 155 use this spoofable value.
findings.impact: IP-based admin access restrictions are ineffective against attackers who can send requests directly to port 4444.

negative_findings.title: No Java Deserialization Surface
negative_findings.description: Exhaustive search found ZERO instances of ObjectInputStream, readObject, readUnshared, XMLDecoder, XStream, or SnakeYAML in the application code. While commons-beanutils-1.9.2 provides an unprotected BeanComparator gadget, and commons-collections-3.2.2 gadgets are present (but hardened with serialization checks), there is no reachable deserialization sink. CSCClient socket communication uses raw byte protocol, not Java serialization. The deserialization attack vector is CLOSED.
negative_findings.impact: None. Gadget libraries are inert without a sink.

negative_findings.title: No XXE in XML Parsers
negative_findings.description: Both XML-parsing classes (DLPPolicy, ControlCenterHelper) use SAXParserFactory/DocumentBuilderFactory with FEATURE_SECURE_PROCESSING=true and accessExternalDTD/accessExternalSchema disabled. Neither is in the SSO/SAML path. SAML/OIDC protocol handling is entirely in Go binaries (oauth2-proxy, access-manager), not Java.

negative_findings.title: Mode 6000 Token Validation Is Server-Side (Cloud)
negative_findings.description: Hammer token validation is delegated to Sophos Central cloud API at {pic_uri}/sophos/api/v2/firewalls/token. The firewall has no local token secret -- brute-forcing must beat Sophos Central's rate limiting, not just the local firewall. The attack surface is limited to the token transport and session creation logic, not the validation itself.
negative_findings.impact: Token brute-force is infeasible against cloud validation.

summary: The parallel deep-dive yielded 5 new findings (SFOS-015 through SFOS-019) and 3 important negative results. The most significant finding is SFOS-016 (X-Forwarded-Email privilege escalation via SSO) which could allow any SSO user to escalate to super-admin. The missing-return bugs in SSOAdminServlet (SFOS-015, SFOS-017) are classic Java logic errors. The deserialization surface is definitively closed (no sinks despite gadget availability). XXE is properly mitigated.
